title: Flow & Kanban Systems
summary: Use Kanban to visualize work, limit work in progress, improve flow, and deliver value predictably.
intro: Kanban is less about assigning tasks and more about managing the flow of value through a visible system. For the PMP exam, focus on transparency, WIP limits, pull-based work, service delivery, and continuous improvement.

sections.heading: Kanban as a Flow System
sections.body:
Kanban helps teams see how work moves from request to delivery. The board is a model of the workflow, not just a task list.

A strong Kanban system makes policies visible, exposes bottlenecks, and gives the team data for improving delivery. It supports adaptive work because the team can respond to changing demand without abandoning discipline.
sections.tip: On the exam, Kanban is usually tied to visual management, limiting WIP, pull systems, and continuous improvement.

groups.label: What
lines: Represent work items and workflow states on a shared board.

groups.label: Use
lines: Create transparency so the team can see progress, waiting work, blocked work, and bottlenecks.

groups.label: What
lines: Observe how work moves through the system over time.

groups.label: Use
lines: Improve predictability, reduce delays, and address constraints where work piles up.

groups.label: What
lines: Use evidence from the workflow to adjust policies and practices.

groups.label: Use
lines: Make small, evolutionary changes rather than large disruptive process changes.

sections.heading: Boards, Cards, and Explicit Policies
sections.body:
A Kanban board divides work into states such as ready, in progress, review, and done. Cards represent individual work items and move as the work advances.

The board is most useful when the team also defines explicit policies. These policies explain how work enters the system, what each column means, when work can move forward, what done means, and how exceptions are handled.

rows.value: The steps work follows from request to delivery.
Enough information to understand scope, owner, priority, due date, and current status.
Rules for pulling work, starting work, finishing work, handling blocked items, and applying WIP limits.
Blocked, urgent, aging, or high-risk items that need attention.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: A board without agreed policies can create visibility but not control. PMP questions often reward the answer that makes work and rules transparent before forcing a solution.

sections.heading: WIP Limits and Pull
sections.body:
Work in progress limits cap the number of items allowed in a workflow state or across a team. The purpose is not to keep everyone busy. The purpose is to finish work faster by reducing multitasking, queues, and hidden overload.

A pull system means team members start new work only when capacity is available. Work is pulled from an upstream queue instead of pushed onto people regardless of capacity.

groups.label: What
lines: A maximum number of active work items in a stage or system.

groups.label: Use
lines: Expose bottlenecks, reduce task switching, and encourage finishing before starting.

groups.label: What
lines: New work starts when the team has capacity.

groups.label: Use
lines: Match demand to capacity and protect the team from unmanaged overload.

groups.label: What
lines: Too many active items compete for attention.

groups.label: Use
lines: Treat it as a system signal: stop starting, help finish, and remove constraints.

sections.heading: Commitment, Delivery, and Flow Metrics
sections.body:
Kanban uses clear points in the workflow to measure performance. The commitment point is where the team accepts work into the system. The delivery point is where the item is complete from the customer or stakeholder perspective.

Metrics should support better decisions, not blame. Lead time, cycle time, throughput, and WIP age help the team understand predictability, delay, and whether work is aging in the system.

items.text: Elapsed time from commitment to delivery, often viewed from the customer perspective.
Elapsed time work spends actively inside a defined part of the workflow.
Number of work items completed in a period.
How long an unfinished item has been in progress.

items.formula: Lead time = delivery point - commitment point
lines: Long lead time may indicate waiting, rework, or overloaded WIP.
Stable throughput improves short-term forecasting.

sections.heading: Blocked Work and Bottlenecks
sections.body:
A blocked item is work that cannot move because of an impediment, dependency, missing information, approval delay, or resource constraint. Blocked items should be obvious on the board so the team can swarm, escalate, or change policy.

A bottleneck is a workflow constraint where work accumulates faster than it can be completed. Kanban treats bottlenecks as system problems, not individual failures.

blocks.title: Respond to blocked or stuck work

items.title: Make it visible
lines: Mark the item clearly and record why it is blocked.

items.title: Protect flow
lines: Avoid pulling more work into an already constrained area.

items.title: Remove the impediment
lines: Clarify requirements, secure a decision, resolve a dependency, or escalate when needed.

items.title: Learn from recurrence
lines: If the same blockage repeats, adjust policy, staffing, sequencing, or stakeholder engagement.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: When a flow problem appears, prefer transparency, collaboration, and impediment removal over assigning blame or simply adding more work.

sections.heading: Classes of Service
sections.body:
Classes of service help teams apply different handling rules to different types of work. They should be explicit so urgent work does not silently disrupt normal work.

The PMP exam may describe a team balancing fixed deadlines, standard requests, emergency work, and lower-urgency improvements. The best answer usually protects flow while honoring real business priority.

rows.value: Urgent, high-value, or time-critical work that may bypass normal order. Use sparingly because it disrupts flow.
Work that must be delivered by a specific date, such as a regulatory or event-driven need.
Normal work handled through the usual policies and priority rules.
Useful work without immediate visible payoff, such as improvement, risk reduction, or technical health.

sections.heading: Cadences and Feedback Loops
sections.body:
Kanban teams use recurring cadences to keep work aligned and improve the system. These events are not the same as Scrum events, but they serve similar goals: inspect reality, coordinate work, and adapt.

Cadences should create useful feedback from the team, customers, and performance data. The purpose is to improve service delivery and maintain customer focus.

rows.value: Review the board, blocked items, aging work, and flow risks.
Decide what work is ready to enter the system when capacity is available.
Assess whether completed work met customer and stakeholder expectations.
Use metrics and team learning to adjust policies, limits, and workflow design.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Feedback loops are a PMBOK-aligned way to support adaptation, stakeholder engagement, quality, and value delivery.

sections.heading: Customer-Focused Service Delivery
sections.body:
Kanban frames work as service delivery. The team is not only completing tasks; it is delivering value to customers or stakeholders through a managed system.

Customer focus means prioritizing valuable work, delivering in smaller increments where appropriate, listening to feedback, and improving the workflow so value arrives sooner and more predictably.

groups.label: What
lines: Work should contribute to outcomes stakeholders care about.

groups.label: Use
lines: Prioritize based on value, urgency, risk, and feedback.

groups.label: What
lines: Customers, users, sponsors, and decision makers shape what success means.

groups.label: Use
lines: Keep feedback visible and use it to refine priorities and acceptance expectations.

groups.label: What
lines: The process evolves as the team learns.

groups.label: Use
lines: Adjust WIP limits, workflow steps, and policies using evidence.

source: synthesized from PMP concept study notes; PMBOK 7/8 aligned
